metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the live compiled state (not just SNAPSHOT output) also carries a `state["signature"]` mirror equal to the most-recent attestation, so the four existing `state.get("signature")` consumers in signing.py, txn.py, and validate.py continue to work without modification in this slice.
findings.finding: The plan says 'New code never writes state["signature"] into events' and describes the legacy `signature` mirror only in new SNAPSHOT output — but it does not specify whether the live compiled state (between signing and compaction) also carries a `state["signature"]` mirror. Four existing call sites read `state.get("signature")` directly from live reducer output (signing.py:423 retire_attested_pin, signing.py:467 verify_signature, txn.py:305 close gate, validate.py:114 fsck). If only SNAPSHOT bytes carry the mirror and not the in-memory compiled state, all four consumers silently break for any ticket that has been signed but not yet compacted — making the close gate return unsigned/not-verified immediately after signing.
findings.location: ## What — 'New code never writes state["signature"] into events; Read fold-in…'; ## Scope — `_processors.py process_signature`
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly that `process_signature` writes BOTH `state["attestations"][kind]` AND keeps `state["signature"]` as a mirror of the most-recent attestation in live compiled state (not only in SNAPSHOT output). This mirrors the SNAPSHOT mirror rationale but at the reducer level, and means the four existing consumers need no change in this slice while the new `attestations` map is wired up.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test (and/or assertion in compact.py) verifying that every new SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state` includes a `signature` mirror whenever `attestations` is non-empty, so a coding error in mirror-synthesis cannot silently omit it and leave a rollback path with data loss.
findings.finding: The `signature` mirror in new SNAPSHOTs is the SOLE rollback safety mechanism — old-code clones after a rollback read `state['signature']` from the mirror in any newly-compacted SNAPSHOT. But the plan specifies no test or assertion ensuring the mirror is ALWAYS present when compact.py writes a new SNAPSHOT (e.g. the 'most-recent attestation' selection could silently yield nothing if `attestations` is empty or all entries are malformed). If even one newly-written SNAPSHOT is missing the mirror, a subsequent rollback to old code means the original SIGNATURE events are gone (deleted by compaction) with no recovery path.
findings.location: AC: 'New SNAPSHOTs synthesize a legacy `signature` mirror (most-recent attestation); kept permanently here.' / src/rebar/_commands/compact.py (mirror synthesis site)
findings.suggested_fix: In compact.py, after synthesizing `compiled_state`, add an assertion: `assert 'signature' in compiled_state or not compiled_state.get('attestations'), 'BUG: attestations present but signature mirror missing — refusing to compact'`. Add a test covering a ticket with one SIGNATURE event that checks the SNAPSHOT compiled_state contains both `attestations` and a matching `signature` mirror.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the bifurcated-attestation-state decision: why the legacy `signature` mirror is synthesized, what the fold-in-on-read contract is, and what triggers its retirement in the follow-up epic.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (bifurcated attestation state with legacy-mirror synthesis + fold-in-on-read) and explicitly defers dropping the mirror to a 'contract-phase follow-up', but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-attestations-map-migration.md` documenting: (1) the per-key-LWW `attestations` contract, (2) the legacy mirror synthesis rationale and its explicitly temporary status, (3) the fold-in-on-read invariant, and reference it from CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all consumers of `last_reopened_at` (including show/MCP/audit) and confirm each handles the absent/null case; add a named trigger condition or guard for retiring the legacy `signature` mirror in the follow-up epic.
findings.finding: Consumers of `last_reopened_at` are under-enumerated: only `compute_validity` is named, and no retirement/cleanup path is specified for the legacy `signature` mirror within this epic, creating a lifecycle gap for both new state fields.
findings.location: ## What — `state['last_reopened_at']`; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (mirror kept permanently)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` to the Scope section so the `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` change is unambiguously required in this story's PR.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists only `_processors.py`, `compact.py`, and `ticket_state.schema.json`, but the 'Added (review)' section explicitly requires changes to `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` (`_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS`) in this story. An executor reading Scope would not include `_present.py` in the changeset.
findings.suggested_fix: Append `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` (strip HMAC hex at `attestations.<kind>.signature` in `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS`) to the Scope file list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` to the Scope file list so the _INTERNAL_SUBKEYS / HMAC-stripping deliverable has explicit scope coverage in this story.
findings.finding: The 'Added (review)' section mandates updating `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` (_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS) as a deliverable in this story, but the Scope section omits this file entirely, leaving an orphan deliverable with no scope coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: Append `src/rebar/reducer/_present.py` (_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS strip for `attestations.<kind>.signature`) to the Scope section to match the 'Added (review)' deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe the mechanism change needed in `public_state()` to strip `signature` from each value-dict inside `state['attestations']` (two-level iteration), not just from a single nested dict at `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` depth.
findings.finding: The `_present.py` HMAC-strip mechanism (`_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS`) operates one level deep — it strips `state['signature']['signature']` by iterating nested dicts keyed by a top-level state key. The new `attestations` shape is `state['attestations'][kind]['signature']` (two levels deep). The existing `public_state()` loop body `{k: v for k, v in nested.items() if k not in subkeys}` can only strip subkeys from the dict at `state['attestations']`, not from each `state['attestations'][kind]` dict inside it. The plan's 'Added (review)' AC correctly scopes the fix here, but says only 'Update `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS`' without describing the required mechanism change — the current loop is structurally insufficient for two-level stripping.
findings.suggested_fix: In `public_state()`, after the existing `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` loop, add a dedicated pass: if `'attestations'` is in `out` and is a dict, rebuild it as `{kind: {k: v for k, v in rec.items() if k != 'signature'} for kind, rec in out['attestations'].items() if isinstance(rec, dict)}`. This is simpler than trying to extend `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` to handle variable-depth nesting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit ACs (or a testing note) for failure/invalid paths in the new routing and fold-in logic: unknown/missing `kind`, unparseable `manifest[0]`, and the zero-attestation empty-map case.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new routing logic (`data["kind"]` with `manifest[0]` fallback) and a legacy fold-in path, both of which have meaningful failure/invalid scenarios (malformed manifest, missing or unrecognised kind, signature with no parseable kind for fold-in), yet the ACs and plan body describe only the happy path with no failure, invalid-input, or empty-attestations-map test cases called out.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1, AC2 and 'process_signature routes by data["kind"] (fallback: parse manifest[0])' in What section
findings.suggested_fix: Add ACs such as: 'process_signature with no data["kind"] and no parseable manifest[0] raises a documented error or routes to a "unknown" fallback kind (tested)'; 'fold-in of a legacy signature whose manifest yields no recognisable kind is handled gracefully (tested)'; 'compaction of a ticket with zero attestations omits the key entirely (tested with an empty-state fixture)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document or define the manifest-prefix convention for every current and planned attestation kind (e.g. add a `KIND_PREFIXES` registry or table), and verify the `manifest[0]` fallback correctly resolves each kind before merging.
findings.finding: The `manifest[0]` fallback for kind-routing is asserted to work generically, but only one kind prefix convention (`'plan-review:'` → `_MANIFEST_PREFIX` in `attest.py`) is actually defined anywhere in the codebase. A second attestation kind (e.g. a future `'completion'` kind) would need an independently established prefix for the fallback to route it correctly — but no such convention or registry is cited in the plan.
findings.location: AC1: 'routing by `data["kind"]` with `manifest[0]` fallback'; What section: '`process_signature` routes by `data["kind"]` (fallback: parse `manifest[0]`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_KIND_PREFIX_MAP: dict[str, str]` in `_processors.py` (or a shared constants module) mapping each known manifest prefix to its kind string (e.g. `{'plan-review:': 'plan-review'}`), and use that registry in the fallback instead of ad-hoc parsing, so new kinds are forced to register their prefix explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC 1 (or a new AC) an explicit test artifact requirement: a unit test or snapshot fixture demonstrating that two process_signature calls with different kinds both survive in `attestations` and neither clobbers the other.
findings.finding: The plan does not provide a concrete end-state-observable proof (e.g., a test scenario or snapshot fixture) for the 'additive across kinds, replace within a kind' invariant — only prose description, making it hard to verify correctness mechanically.
findings.suggested_fix: AC 1 addition: 'Verified by a unit test: given two process_signature events with kind=A and kind=B, compiled state["attestations"] contains both keys; a third event with kind=A replaces only kind=A.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for materializing `last_reopened_at` in compiled state (e.g. avoids O(event-log) scan at every verify call; mirrors how `parent_status_uuid` avoids re-scanning for fork resolution) and clarify whether `compute_validity` is introduced in this story or a later slice.
findings.finding: The plan adds `state['last_reopened_at']` and names `compute_validity` as its consumer, but `compute_validity` does not exist anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via search), and the plan does not state whether it is introduced in this story or a later slice. More critically, the plan provides no positive rationale for WHY this check should be materialized into compiled state (reducer) rather than derived on-demand at verify time by scanning event timestamps — the alternative a reviewer would naturally consider.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the HMAC AC: either mark it `[x]` (already satisfied by 114f, no action needed here) or remove the '(closed in 114f)' annotation and confirm it must be delivered in this story alongside the `_present.py` scope addition.
findings.finding: The HMAC AC checkbox in 'Added (review)' is listed as an open `- [ ]` item but is annotated '(closed in 114f)', creating a contradiction: the plan simultaneously requires this work here and declares it already done elsewhere.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the ordering criterion for 'most-recent attestation' in the legacy mirror (e.g., latest `signed_at` timestamp, or last event in journal order) so AC3 is unambiguously evaluable.
findings.finding: The legacy `signature` mirror is defined as 'most-recent attestation' but the plan does not specify the ordering criterion (event sequence, `signed_at` timestamp, or kind priority) used to select the most-recent one when multiple attestation kinds exist.
findings.location: ## What — 'a NEW snapshot ALSO synthesizes a legacy signature (= most-recent attestation)'; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'most-recent = highest `signed_at` timestamp across all kinds; ties broken by journal event order (last wins).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and explicitly update or remove any existing tests that assert on `state["signature"]` as the primary write target, and note this test-maintenance work in the plan or ACs.
findings.finding: The old `state["signature"]` single-slot write path is being deleted/replaced, but the plan names no existing tests to modify or remove, leaving open whether stale tests that assert on `state["signature"]` in events will silently continue to pass against the new shape or will be orphaned.
findings.location: AC2 ('New code never writes state["signature"] into events') and Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'Existing tests for process_signature asserting state["signature"] are updated to assert state["attestations"][<kind>] instead; the legacy mirror is covered by a separate, clearly-labelled backward-compat test that is marked for deletion in the follow-up epic slice.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split `last_reopened_at` tracking (and its `_present.py` HMAC-stripping AC) into a separate leaf ticket, or explicitly justify the bundling with a shared schema migration rationale that makes decomposition impractical.
findings.finding: The plan bundles two structurally independent goals — (1) the attestations{} kind-keyed map and (2) `state['last_reopened_at']` for closed→open transition tracking — in a single ticket without child decomposition, despite touching ≥4 files across ≥3 layers.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a child ticket 'Reducer: add last_reopened_at for closed→open transitions' containing process_status changes, the schema field, and the compute_validity dependency. The attestations{} ticket retains only the map replacement, fold-in, mirror, and compaction round-trip. The HMAC stripping in _present.py can be a one-liner follow-on in the same PR as attestations or its own AC if it truly closes a leak window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or spec clause defining the merge/precedence rule when a snapshot contains BOTH `signature` and `attestations` (e.g., `attestations` wins and fold-in is skipped, or fold-in is always additive/idempotent with explicit kind-keyed dedup).
findings.finding: The fold-in-on-read path (legacy `signature` → `attestations`) doesn't specify behavior when a snapshot already contains BOTH `signature` AND `attestations` keys — possible during a partial migration or after a rollback — leaving a potential double-fold or conflict unaddressed.
findings.location: ## What — 'Read fold-in: a ticket/snapshot carrying only the legacy `signature` is folded into `attestations` on read'
findings.suggested_fix: In `process_snapshot`: if `attestations` key already present in the snapshot, skip fold-in of `signature` entirely (attestations-wins rule); document this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / back-out note stating that (a) the legacy `signature` mirror in snapshots is the rollback-safety mechanism, (b) the mirror MUST be preserved until the deferred contract phase, and (c) reverting this code while retaining new-format snapshots is safe precisely because the mirror is present.
findings.finding: The plan's expand-contract mechanism (the legacy `signature` mirror in new snapshots) provides rollback safety, but this is never explicitly framed as a rollback/back-out path — the mirror's role as the reversibility mechanism is implicit, leaving implementers without a documented back-out procedure if this slice needs to be reverted.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
